texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract Wepresent the results of a statistical study of the distribution of mirror and Alfvén-ion cyclotron (AIC) waves in the magnetosheath together with plasma parameters important for the stability of ULF waves, specifically ion temperature anisotropy and ion beta. Magnetosheath crossings registered by Cluster spacecraft over the course of 2 years served as a basis for the statistics. For each observation we used bow shock, magnetopause, and magnetosheath flow models to identify the relative position of the spacecraft with respect to magnetosheath boundaries and local properties of the upstream shock crossing. A strong dependence of both plasma parameters and mirror/AIC wave occurrence on upstream Θ Bn and MA is identified. We analyzed a joint dependence of the same parameters on Θ Bn and fractional distance between shock and magnetopause, zenith angle, and length of the flow line. Finally, the occurrence of mirror and AIC modes was compared against the respective instability thresholds. We noted that AIC waves occurred nearly exclusively under mirror stable conditions. This is interpreted in terms of different characters of nonlinear saturation of the two modes.
texts.text: Abstract Wepresent the results of a statistical study of the distribution of mirror and Alfvén-ion cyclotron (AIC) waves in the magnetosheath together with plasma parameters important for the stability of ULF waves, specifically ion temperature anisotropy and ion beta. Magnetosheath crossings registered by Cluster spacecraft over the course of 2 years served as a basis for the statistics. For each observation we used bow shock, magnetopause, and magnetosheath flow models to identify the relative position of the spacecraft with respect to magnetosheath boundaries and local properties of the upstream shock crossing. A strong dependence of both plasma parameters and mirror/AIC wave occurrence on upstream Θ Bn and MA is identified. We analyzed a joint dependence of the same parameters on Θ Bn and fractional distance between shock and magnetopause, zenith angle, and length of the flow line. Finally, the occurrence of mirror and AIC modes was compared against the respective instability thresholds. We noted that AIC waves occurred nearly exclusively under mirror stable conditions. This is interpreted in terms of different characters of nonlinear saturation of the two modes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this article we address the problem of spatial distribution of ultralow frequency (ULF) waves and plasma parameters determining the stability of plasma in the terrestrial magnetosheath. Based on a large statistical data set, we studied the relative occurrence of the waves in relation to the parameters of the upstream shock crossing and the location of the observation within the magnetosheath.

texts.orig: Thespatial distribution of the ULF waves in the magnetosheath has also been studied previously. It was shown that the AIC waves are found predominantly near the bow shock [ Song et al. , 1994; Hubert et al. , 1998] and close to the magnetopause and, in particular, in the plasma depletion layer [ Anderson and Fuselier , 1993], while mirror structures dominate in the central and downstream magnetosheath [ Génot et al. , 2009a; Verigin et al. , 2006] but can occur immediately downstream from the shock too [ Hubert et al. , 1989]. The above studies also showed that the ULF fluctuations are generally stronger in the dayside magnetosheath. Anderson and Fuselier [1993] performed a small-scale statistical study of the dependence of the observed ULF waves on the upstream shock type. They distinguished between quasi-perpendicular (Q ⊥ ) and quasi-parallel (Q ‖ ) magnetosheath based on a flux of locally observed energetic He ++ ions which is known to be stronger behind Q ‖ shock. This coarse technique allowed to establish that mostly AIC waves were observed in Q ‖ magnetosheath, while in the Q ⊥ magnetosheath the ratio of mirror and AIC observations was close to 1. In their data set, the occurrence rate of either type of wave was lower in Q ‖ magnetosheath.

texts.orig: Themirror structures have received considerable attention recently, when their nonlinear behavior and shape have been addressed. Mirror structures are known to appear in several forms: mirror peaks, dips, and sometimes more irregular or quasi-sinusoidal structures. Several statistical and numerical studies described the relative occurrence of peaks and dips as a function of local plasma parameters and location within magnetosheath [ Soucek et al. , 2008; Califano et al. , 2008; Génot et al. , 2009b, 2011]. It was shown that mirror peaks only appear in mirror unstable plasma ( R > 1), most often in central dayside magnetosheath, while dips can exist for even lower values of R and dominate in low/u1D6FD plasma, such as in the magnetosheath flanks and close to the magnetopause.

Several statistical and numerical studies described the relative occurrence of peaks and dips as a function of local plasma parameters and location within magnetosheath [ Soucek et al. , 2008; Califano et al. , 2008; Génot et al. , 2009b, 2011]. texts.orig: The character of locally generated ULF waves is determined by local plasma properties which are largely affected by the process of heating and compression of solar wind plasma taking place at the bow shock. From Rankine-Hugoniot relations [ Chao et al. , 1995; Génot , 2009] it can be shown that downstream temperature anisotropy and /u1D6FD are determined by solar wind parameters and the local properties of the shock: the angle Θ Bn between the shock normal and upstream interplanetary magnetic field (IMF) and local shock Mach number (in this work, we used Alfvénic Mach number MA ). This is true for magnetosheath plasma near the bow shock; further downstream in the magnetosheath, other effects such as expansions or instabilities affect the plasma conditions.

texts.orig: This dependence naturally results in an asymmetric distribution of plasma parameters and waves in the magnetosheath driven by the IMF direction and can be identified in statistical studies as dawn-dusk asymmetry due to the typical Parker spiral orientation of IMF. A number of studies summarized, for example, in a recent review article by Walsh et al. [2014] have shown dawn-dusk asymmetries in plasma density, velocity, magnetic field magnitude, and in the nature of magnetic field fluctuations. The dawn-dusk asymmetry dependent on the IMF orientation is conveniently characterized in the magnetosheath interplanetary medium (MIPM) reference frame [ Verigin et al. , 2006], where the dawnside is always connected to a Q ‖ shock and duskside to Q ⊥ .

The dawn-dusk asymmetry dependent on the IMF orientation is conveniently characterized in the magnetosheath interplanetary medium (MIPM) reference frame [ Verigin et al. , 2006], where the dawnside is always connected to a Q ‖ shock and duskside to Q ⊥ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the MIPM frame Dimmock and Nykyri [2013] showed that the dawn magnetosheath contains stronger magnetic field and faster plasma flow, but no significant asymmetry in density was found in contrast to other studies such as Paularena et al. [2001] and Walsh et al. [2012]. Statistical studies have shown that the overall level of magnetic field oscillations is larger on the Q ‖ sides of the magnetosheath [ Luhmann et al. , 1986; Dimmocketal. , 2014] and that there is a qualitative difference in the nature of oscillations [ Shevyrevetal. , 2007; Petrinec , 2013]. On the Q ‖ side, the field direction fluctuates on the time scale of minutes, corresponding to turbulence originating from the Q ‖ shock and possibly foreshock. The Q ⊥ side is populated to a larger extent by smaller-scale oscillation of compressional nature, corresponding to locally generated waves.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Data Set and Data Processing
texts.text: 2. Data Set and Data Processing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present work is based on a statistical data set of in situ plasma and field measurements performed in the magnetosheath by Cluster spacecraft [ Escoubet et al. , 2001]. The data set used in this study covers two years of magnetosheath observations. All intervals when Cluster 1 spacecraft was located in the magnetosheath between dates 4 November 2006 and 10 June 2008 are included. These two years were chosen because of a favorable Cluster orbit: during this interval, the apogee was drifting south of the ecliptic plane and the orbital planewasrotatingtowardequatorialorientationresultinginagoodcoverageofthesubsolarmagnetosheath. Magnetosheath interval selection was performed by interactive visual identification of bow shock and magnetosheath crossings based on magnetic field magnitude and ion energy spectra. Two Cluster data sets were used in this study-magnetic field intensity sampled at five vectors per second from the FGM magnetometer [ Balogh et al. , 2001] and onboard calculated ion moments (density, flow velocity, and perpendicular and parallel temperatures) from the Cluster Ion Spectrometry-Hot Ion Analyzer ion spectrometer [ Rème et al. , 1997]. This instrument does not separate individual ion species; therefore, the obtained ion moments are an approximation of H + contaminated by the presence of He ++ . In a majority of cases, the He ++ abundance is low and the moments can be considered a good approximation of H + moments. A study dedicated to the He ++ population is beyond the scope of this paper, but it would represent a valuable complement to our results. All Cluster data were obtained from the public Cluster Science Archive.
texts.text: The present work is based on a statistical data set of in situ plasma and field measurements performed in the magnetosheath by Cluster spacecraft [ Escoubet et al. , 2001]. The data set used in this study covers two years of magnetosheath observations. All intervals when Cluster 1 spacecraft was located in the magnetosheath between dates 4 November 2006 and 10 June 2008 are included. These two years were chosen because of a favorable Cluster orbit: during this interval, the apogee was drifting south of the ecliptic plane and the orbital planewasrotatingtowardequatorialorientationresultinginagoodcoverageofthesubsolarmagnetosheath. Magnetosheath interval selection was performed by interactive visual identification of bow shock and magnetosheath crossings based on magnetic field magnitude and ion energy spectra. Two Cluster data sets were used in this study-magnetic field intensity sampled at five vectors per second from the FGM magnetometer [ Balogh et al. , 2001] and onboard calculated ion moments (density, flow velocity, and perpendicular and parallel temperatures) from the Cluster Ion Spectrometry-Hot Ion Analyzer ion spectrometer [ Rème et al. , 1997]. This instrument does not separate individual ion species; therefore, the obtained ion moments are an approximation of H + contaminated by the presence of He ++ . In a majority of cases, the He ++ abundance is low and the moments can be considered a good approximation of H + moments. A study dedicated to the He ++ population is beyond the scope of this paper, but it would represent a valuable complement to our results. All Cluster data were obtained from the public Cluster Science Archive.

texts.orig: The magnetosheath data were split in 5 min long intervals, and a set of field and plasma parameters was calculated on each 5 min interval. This length of interval was chosen with regard to the characteristic scale of mirror and ion cyclotron waves whose frequencies are typically between 0.05 Hz and 1 Hz. For each interval, wecalculatedaveragevaluesofionmoments,fieldmagnitude,andderivedquantitiessuchasionbetaparameter and temperature anisotropy. Intervals with missing data, low-quality data, and intervals where the CIS instrument was not in a mode appropriate for magnetosheath plasma measurements were removed. Unless specified otherwise, all data presented in this paper are calculated as averages over 5 min intervals and one 5 min interval constitutes one data point in the statistics. This way we obtained a set of 26,948 data points. The distribution of the data points in GSE coordinates with respect to average magnetosheath boundaries are shown in Figure 1. The plot demonstrates a good coverage of dayside magnetosheath near the equatorial plane and in the Southern Hemisphere.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Each interval was scanned for the presence of ULF waves using an automated technique based on variance analysis of magnetic field. Previous research discussed in section 1 has shown that two wave modes are responsible for a large majority of magnetosheath ULF waves: the mirror mode waves and Alfvén-ion cyclotron waves. In observed magnetic field data, one can distinguish between these two modes relatively easily based on their different polarization [ Krauss-Varban et al. , 1994; Song et al. , 1994]. Magnetic field variations corresponding to mirror waves are typically linearly polarized with the variation /u1D6FF B nearly parallel to background field B . Ion cyclotron waves, on the other hand, are known to be transversely left-hand polarized with the k vector directed obliquely or nearly parallel to the background field [ Remya et al. , 2014].
texts.text: Each interval was scanned for the presence of ULF waves using an automated technique based on variance analysis of magnetic field. Previous research discussed in section 1 has shown that two wave modes are responsible for a large majority of magnetosheath ULF waves: the mirror mode waves and Alfvén-ion cyclotron waves. In observed magnetic field data, one can distinguish between these two modes relatively easily based on their different polarization [ Krauss-Varban et al. , 1994; Song et al. , 1994]. Magnetic field variations corresponding to mirror waves are typically linearly polarized with the variation /u1D6FF B nearly parallel to background field B . Ion cyclotron waves, on the other hand, are known to be transversely left-hand polarized with the k vector directed obliquely or nearly parallel to the background field [ Remya et al. , 2014].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Each interval of data is analyzed using standard variance analysis [ Sonnerup and Sheible , 1998] to obtain the minimum and maximum variance direction vectors. We also transform the magnetic field waveform to field-aligned coordinates (using mean field vector ̄ B to obtain RMS values of fluctuations in ̄ B parallel and perpendicular direction: /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ and /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ . We now classify as mirror events those intervals, where /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ ∕ ̄ B 2 > 0 . 1, /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ ∕ /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ > 2, and the angle between ̄ B and the maximum variance direction is less than 25 ∘ . This criterion is similar to that used, for example, by Génot et al. [2009b]. Analogously, intervals are identified as AIC events when /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ ∕ ̄ B 2 > 0 . 1, /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ ∕ /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ < 0 . 5, and the angle between ̄ B and the minimum variance direction is less than 45 ∘ . The mirror intervals are further classified as mirror peaks or mirror dips based on the value of normalized skewness S of the magnetic field magnitude | B | [ Génot et al. , 2009b], providing a simple and easily reproducible criterion. Mirror intervals where S > 0 . 1 are classified as peaks and intervals where S < -0 . 1 as dips.
texts.text: Each interval of data is analyzed using standard variance analysis [ Sonnerup and Sheible , 1998] to obtain the minimum and maximum variance direction vectors. We also transform the magnetic field waveform to field-aligned coordinates (using mean field vector ̄ B to obtain RMS values of fluctuations in ̄ B parallel and perpendicular direction: /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ and /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ . We now classify as mirror events those intervals, where /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ ∕ ̄ B 2 > 0 . 1, /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ ∕ /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ > 2, and the angle between ̄ B and the maximum variance direction is less than 25 ∘ . This criterion is similar to that used, for example, by Génot et al. [2009b]. Analogously, intervals are identified as AIC events when /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ ∕ ̄ B 2 > 0 . 1, /u1D6FF B 2 ‖ ∕ /u1D6FF B 2 ⊥ < 0 . 5, and the angle between ̄ B and the minimum variance direction is less than 45 ∘ . The mirror intervals are further classified as mirror peaks or mirror dips based on the value of normalized skewness S of the magnetic field magnitude | B | [ Génot et al. , 2009b], providing a simple and easily reproducible criterion. Mirror intervals where S > 0 . 1 are classified as peaks and intervals where S < -0 . 1 as dips.

texts.orig: It should be noted that this simple method suitable for large-scale studies does not identify other transverse wave modes that might be present (such as the fast MHD mode), and these are classified as AIC waves. Mixed events containing a signature of both mirror and AIC waves are not resolved either.

texts.orig: The dependence of the investigated plasma parameters on the properties of the upstream shock crossing is shown in Figure 4. Color coded are median values of A , ion /u1D6FD ‖ , and R calculated from data points binned by Θ Bn and MA . The strong dependence of all three plasma parameters on the shock properties is evident: the strongest anisotropy is observed at quasi-perpendicular shocks with low Mach number, while quasi-parallel shocks with large Mach number are correlated with the highest values of /u1D6FD ‖ . The observed anisotropy-beta anticorrelation can be attributed to the double adiabatic fluid theory Chew-Goldberger-Low (CGL) law which predicts an inverse dependence between A and /u1D6FD ‖ during adiabatic plasma compression or expansion. CGL lawcanbeappliedtomagnetosheathplasmaexpansionontheglobalscale,butbothmirrorandioncyclotron instabilities are known to constrain this relationship by dissipating excess anisotropy [ Anderson and Fuselier , 1993; Fuselier et al. , 1994].

texts.orig: In Figure 5 is presented a statistics of ULF wave occurrence as a function of the same shock parameters as in Figure 4. Here the color represents the relative occurrence of each type of ULF wave: the ratio of the number of 5 min intervals with identified waves of a specific type to the total number of intervals in the data set falling into this bin. This figure needs to be interpreted together with Figure 4 as the wave occurrence depends strongly on the average plasma parameters. The occurrence of identified mirror structures is plotted for all identified mirror events (Figure 5c), for mirror peaks defined by positive skewness S > 0 . 1 (Figure 5a), and mirror dips with negative skewness S < -0 . 1 (Figure 5b). Mirror peaks are most frequently observed for high Mach number Q ⊥ shock, in the same region where the R parameter (Figure 4b) is the largest. Mirror dips, on the other hand, are distributed more evenly in the parameter space but favor quasi-perpendicular conditions and appear within the region of lower /u1D6FD ‖ and moderate Mach numbers as seen in Figure 4c. The above dependence correlations between R , /u1D6FD ‖ , and mirror wave occurrence agrees well with previous results [ Soucek et al. , 2008], the dependence on MA with results of Génot et al. [2009a], and Θ Bn coarsely agrees with the MIPM frame result of Verigin et al. [2006]. Figure 4d shows the distribution of identified AIC waves.

texts.orig: Interestingly, these waves are clustered in the lowMA part of the histogram for moderate Q ⊥ conditions with most values between 50 ∘ < Θ Bn < 80 ∘ . The observation that significantly more mirror waves are observed in Q ⊥ magnetosheath corresponds well with Anderson and Fuselier [1993], but contrary to their statistics, our results show a lack of AIC observation in the Q ‖ part. It can be seen that the region of parameter space occupied by AIC waves corresponds to low/u1D6FD ‖ plasmas and that AIC waves were found exactly for those shock conditions not favorable for mirror modes. The competition between mirror and ion cyclotron instabilities [ Gary et al. , 1993] is most likely responsible for this effect. As seen in Figure 4, low Mach number shocks result in highly anisotropic, low/u1D6FD plasmas. Under these conditions the mirror instability is usually inhibited ( R < 1) and the AIC waves are more likely to be observed (cf. also section 3.3).
texts.text: Interestingly, these waves are clustered in the lowMA part of the histogram for moderate Q ⊥ conditions with most values between 50 ∘ < Θ Bn < 80 ∘ . The observation that significantly more mirror waves are observed in Q ⊥ magnetosheath corresponds well with Anderson and Fuselier [1993], but contrary to their statistics, our results show a lack of AIC observation in the Q ‖ part. It can be seen that the region of parameter space occupied by AIC waves corresponds to low/u1D6FD ‖ plasmas and that AIC waves were found exactly for those shock conditions not favorable for mirror modes. The competition between mirror and ion cyclotron instabilities [ Gary et al. , 1993] is most likely responsible for this effect. As seen in Figure 4, low Mach number shocks result in highly anisotropic, low/u1D6FD plasmas. Under these conditions the mirror instability is usually inhibited ( R < 1) and the AIC waves are more likely to be observed (cf. also section 3.3).

Mirror dips also show a very strong dependence on l F for all angles of Θ Bn , consistent with Génot et al. [2011]. The distribution of AIC waves shows a more interesting pattern. In all three bottom panels (Figures 7g-7i) we observe that AIC waves generally appear for moderate Q ⊥ conditions (45 ∘ < Θ Bn < 80 ∘ ) as already seen in Figure 5. There are two distinct clusters of AIC wave activity apparent in Figures 7g and 7h: a strong occurrence near the bow shock ( F > 0 . 6) at medium zenith angles 50 ∘ < /u1D717 < 90 ∘ and a weaker patch of AIC waves near the magnetopause, probably corresponding to PDL waves. As seen in Figure 7i, AIC waves are observed for all values of l F with local maxima just behind the shock and for values between 12 and 20 Earth radii. The occurrence of AIC waves behind the shock was noted by Song et al. [1994] and Hubert et al. [1998], and the occurrence in PDL is well documented in Anderson and Fuselier [1993] and Anderson et al. [1994]. Similar to Fuselier et al. [1994], our statistics shows very few AIC waves for Q ‖ conditions. The occurrence rate of AIC waves in the PDL identified in our study is relatively low, possibly due to the fact that some of the waves may appear at frequencies above 2 Hz which are not resolved properly in our data.

texts.orig: A remarkable feature stands out in this figure: the AIC waves are strictly limited to the area below the mirror threshold curve. This can be interpreted in terms of competition between mirror and IC instabilities. AIC waves only appear when mirror instability is inhibited. This can suggest that when linear theory predicts a positive growth rate for both instabilities, the mirror instability dominates despite the fact that the growth rate of ion cyclotron waves should be larger. In the case when mirror instability threshold is not reached, the AIC waves can grow uninhibited.

texts.orig: A convenient explanation is provided by the different saturation properties of the instabilities as demonstrated in three-dimensional hybrid simulations by Shoji et al. [2009]. In their simulation, the authors follow the competition of the two instabilities from initial linear stage to nonlinear saturation. It is shown that while initially, the ion cyclotron stability grows faster, the growth is quickly saturated and the AIC waves are damped after a short time. The mirror waves, on the other hand, while initially growing slower, reach a stable saturated state in which large-amplitude | B | oscillations persist for a long time. This mechanism explains this feature and also the large dominance of mirror modes over AIC waves in magnetosheath observations.

In the studied case of terrestrial magnetosheath the minor ions (mostly He ++ ) are known to affect the competition of the two modes [ Gary et al. , 1993], enabling the mirror waves to grow at lower values of ion /u1D6FD , but they do not seem to play as dominant role as in the case of Io wake. Similar to our results, it was reported [ Russell et al. , 1999; Pokhotelov et al. , 2001] that the growth of mirror structures inhibits the ion cyclotron instability and both types of waves are not observed simultaneously. In these studies, it was postulated that the magnetic field gradients associated with mirror structures perturb the gyromotion of the ions and remove the ions from the cyclotron resonance. This mechanism provides an alternative explanation of the effect observed in our data and is consistent with the simulations of Shoji et al. [2009].

texts.orig: 7. Both mirror and AIC waves follow their respective thresholds, but AIC waves were detected only below the mirror threshold. We interpret this effect as the result of a different mode of saturation of AIC and mirror instabilities in the nonlinear stage as described by Shoji et al. [2009]. After the instability reduces the plasma to a stable state, AIC waves are damped after a short time, while mirror instability saturates in the form of persistent oscillatory structures in the magnetic field which are much more likely to be observed.
texts.text: Both mirror and AIC waves follow their respective thresholds, but AIC waves were detected only below the mirror threshold. We interpret this effect as the result of a different mode of saturation of AIC and mirror instabilities in the nonlinear stage as described by Shoji et al. [2009]. After the instability reduces the plasma to a stable state, AIC waves are damped after a short time, while mirror instability saturates in the form of persistent oscillatory structures in the magnetic field which are much more likely to be observed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work presents comprehensive quantitative results on the distribution of ULF waves and ion parameters in the magnetosheath. Our results generally agree with partial results on specific dependencies published previously. The detection of waves performed in this article was adequate for the objective of this study, but the technique can be refined and the data set can be employed to study the detailed ULF wave properties within the magnetosheath. An investigation of analogous plasma parameters corresponding to He ++ ions would also contribute to the understanding of magnetosheath ULF wave generation.
